content.text:
Looking at your tweak in `## In Progress`:

```
[#1](link-to-#1) // bot fix this so it's a link to #1 below
```

I hit a snag making this a working link — two things:

1. **There's no item #1 to link to.** `0.55.0-0` deleted `## Todo` item #1 (the delete-on-pickup rule — and CLAUDE.md calls a `## Todo` entry duplicating `## In Progress` work a "process bug"). `## Todo` now starts at `2.`. The current first item is "single-field `options_flags` leaves" — unrelated to this cycle.

2. **Markdown can't anchor-link to a list item anyway.** GitHub auto-generates anchors only for headings (`## Todo` → `#todo`), not for `1.`/`2.` list entries. A `[text](#1)`-style link to a numbered entry needs an explicit HTML anchor manually placed at that entry.

So `[#1]` has no valid target as written. What did you intend? My read of the options:

- **Link to this cycle's design** — replace the line with a link to the chores-11 opener section (`[validate-todo / fix-todo](chores/chores-11.md#feat-open-validate-todo--fix-todo-0550-0)`). This is the meaningful "what is this In Progress work" pointer. *(My recommendation.)*
- **Drop the line** — the ladder bullets already name the cycle (`0.55.0-0 … chores opener`); In Progress doesn't need a separate header link.
- **Keep item #1 in `## Todo`** and link the In Progress section to it — but that re-introduces the Todo/In-Progress duplication CLAUDE.md flags as a process bug.

Which way do you want it — or did you mean something else by "#1 below"?

# Todo

This file contains near term tasks with a short description
and reference links to more details.

Intro paragraphs in `## Todo` and `## Bugs` below begin every
line with exactly 1 leading space so they don't accidentally match
the renumber script's entry regex (`^\d+\.\s`). Minimum is the
principled choice — the convention's job is to dodge the
column-0 anchor, not to add visual emphasis. See
`notes/fix-todo.py`.

## In Progress

A bulleted list of the in-progress task's development "ladder":
   - 0.xx.y-0 blah (done)
   - 0.xx.y-1 blah blah (current)
   - 0.xx.y-2 blah blah blah
   - 0.xx.y close-out and validation

[#1](link-to-#1) // bot fix this so it's a link to #1 below

- 0.55.0-0 plan + version bump + chores opener (current)
- 0.55.0-1 add validate-todo subcommand (todo_helpers.rs +
  validate_todo.rs)
- 0.55.0-2 add fix-todo subcommand; dogfood it to renumber
  todo.md; delete notes/fix-todo.py; docs sweep
- 0.55.0 close-out

## Todo

 A markdown list of tasks to do in the near future, ordered
 highest-priority first. Keep entries brief — 1-3 lines.
 Detailed motivation, safety requirements, and ordering belong
 in `notes/chores/chores-NN.md` design subsections; link via `[N]` ref.

 Entries are manually numbered (`1.` `2.` …) so the index is
 readable in the raw markdown source. Reorder/insert renumbers the
 entries below — accept the diff churn in exchange for being
 able to count without rendering. Reference by entry number
 ("let's work on #3"). Continuation lines under a two-digit
 entry indent 4 spaces (under `NN. `) rather than 3.

### 1. **`vc-x1 fix-todo` / `validate-todo` subcommands.** Parallel
   shape to `validate-desc` / `fix-desc` (check vs rewrite).
   Renumber `## Todo` and `## Bugs` entries in `notes/todo.md`,
   normalize continuation-line indent to match prefix width.
   Rust replacement for the interim `notes/fix-todo.py`.
   Belongs in the broader "notes/repo hygiene" wave alongside
   `validate-repo` ([[19]]).
2. **single-field `options_flags` leaves → `value` field.**
   `0.47.0` introduced the convention (single-field leaf names
   its field `value`, declares the flag via `#[arg(long = "…")]`,
   so consumers read `args.<leaf>.value` not `args.<leaf>.<leaf>`)
   on the new `squash` leaf. Sweep the pre-existing single-field
   leaves to match: `repo`, `dry_run`, `private`, `account`,
   `config`, `use_template` + their consumers
   (`init.rs`, tests).

   Note: can a single field be defined as an type or enum instead

content.text:
I read the current file — line 44 is now `### 1. **`vc-x1 fix-todo` / `validate-todo` subcommands.** Parallel`.

It half-works. `###` does generate a GitHub anchor, so a link *can* resolve — but as written it breaks in three ways:

1. **The heading is just line 44.** `### ` makes the whole physical line the heading, so the heading text ends `…subcommands.** Parallel` — cut off mid-sentence — and lines 45–50 become an orphaned indented block under it.
2. **`1.` is still in the heading.** `### 1. …` doesn't match the renumber regex `^\d+\. ` (starts with `#`), so `fix-todo` won't treat it as entry 1 — it'll renumber the `2.` entry to `1.`, and you get two "#1"s.
3. **A heading inside the numbered list breaks the section contract.** `## Todo` is defined as a uniform manually-numbered list — and that list is exactly what this cycle's `validate-todo`/`fix-todo` operates on. A `###` item mixed in is a special case the tooling and every reader now has to handle.

So `###` buys an anchor at the cost of the list model. My recommendation instead:

- **Drop the `### `** — restore item #1 as a plain `1.` entry, so `## Todo` stays a uniform `1.`…`44.` list.
- In `## In Progress`, make the link **`[#1](#todo)`** — `#todo` is the auto-anchor for the `## Todo` heading; since #1 is the first entry, the link lands right on it. No HTML hacks, list stays uniform, renumber tooling stays simple.

If you specifically need the link to land *exactly* on item 1's line rather than the section top, the only clean way is to pull item 1 out of the numbered list entirely into a proper standalone `### `vc-x1 fix-todo` / `validate-todo` subcommands` heading + paragraph — but that takes the active task out of the list, and I'd want to think through close-out and the tooling for it.

Either way I'll update CLAUDE.md's delete-on-pickup rule to the keep-#1-and-link model.

Which do you want — plain `1.` + `[#1](#todo)`, or the standalone-heading restructure?

Hmm, the line is:

### 1. **`vc-x1 fix-todo` / `validate-todo` subcommands.** Parallel

Can that be a target from a link

content.text:
Yes. Any `###` heading gets an auto-generated GitHub anchor, so it's a valid link target.

For that exact line, the rendered heading text is `1. vc-x1 fix-todo / validate-todo subcommands. Parallel`, which slugs to:

```
1-vc-x1-fix-todo--validate-todo-subcommands-parallel
```

(lowercase; `.` and `/` dropped; spaces → hyphens; the `--` is the ` / ` with the slash removed). So the link from `## In Progress` would be:

```
[#1](#1-vc-x1-fix-todo--validate-todo-subcommands-parallel)
```

That resolves and works. Two caveats on *that specific line*, though:

- The anchor carries `parallel` and a `--`, because `### ` makes the **whole physical line** the heading — including the trailing word "Parallel" — and keeps the leading `1.`. Ugly but valid.
- It still collides with renumbering: `### 1. …` isn't seen as entry 1 by `fix-todo`, so the `2.` entry below renumbers to `1.` → two "#1"s.

If you want a clean anchor, trimming the heading to just `### `vc-x1 fix-todo` / `validate-todo` subcommands` gives `#vc-x1-fix-todo--validate-todo-subcommands`. Want me to go ahead and wire it up — and if so, with the line as-is or trimmed?

content.text:
Wired — line 21 is now `[1](#current-in-progress)`. `### Current In Progress` slugs to `current-in-progress`: clean, and **stable** — that anchor never changes per cycle, so the In Progress link is permanent. Item #1 stays a normal `1.` entry, so `## Todo` is a uniform `1.`…`44.` list (good — the tooling stays simple; it just skips `###` lines).

But keeping item #1 instead of deleting it makes three things from earlier 0.55.0-0 work stale:

1. **chores-11 opener paragraph** — it currently says `0.55.0-0` *deletes* item 1 and leaves a gap closed by a `-2` dogfood-renumber. None of that holds now. Needs a rewrite: item #1 kept under `### Current In Progress`, `## In Progress` links to it.
2. **`0.55.0-2` ladder bullet** ("dogfood it to renumber todo.md") — the list is already `1.`…`44.`, nothing to renumber at -2. The renumber-dogfood naturally moves to **close-out**: when item #1 → `## Done` it leaves a gap, and `vc-x1 fix-todo --no-dry-run` closes it — a fitting final act for the cycle.
3. **CLAUDE.md's delete-on-pickup rule** — it currently mandates deleting the `## Todo` entry on pickup and calls a Todo/In-Progress duplicate a "process bug." The keep-#1 + `### Current In Progress` + link model contradicts it in ~3 spots (the Versioning section, the chores-10 "Process: delete a Todo item…" subsection, and pre-commit checklist item b).

Shall I reconcile all three as part of `0.55.0-0` so the commit is internally consistent?

259	### Versioning
260	
261	Every plan must start with a version bump. Choose the approach based
262	on scope:
263	
264	- **Single-step** (recommended for mechanical/focused changes): bump
265	  directly to `X.Y.Z`, implement in one commit. Simpler history.
266	- **Multi-step** (for exploratory/large changes): bump to `X.Y.Z-0`,
267	  implement across multiple commits incrementing the numeric
268	  suffix. The final commit drops the suffix.
269	
270	The plan should recommend one approach and get user approval before
271	starting.
272	
273	A plan that picks up a `## Todo` item — or *re-scopes* an
274	existing `## In Progress` ladder to absorb one — **deletes that
275	`## Todo` entry in the same commit** (the version-bump / plan
276	commit). `## In Progress` is the sole record of that work until
277	close-out, when it moves to `## Done`; a cycle that merges
278	several entangled `## Todo` items deletes all of them. A `## Todo`
279	entry that duplicates current `## In Progress` work is a process
280	bug.
281	
282	For multi-step:
283	
284	1. Bump version to `X.Y.Z-0` with the plan and commit as a chore
285	   marker.
286	2. Implement in one or more `X.Y.Z-N` commits (increment N as
287	   needed).
288	3. Final commit bumps to `X.Y.Z` (no suffix), updates
289	   `notes/todo.md` and `notes/chores/chores-*.md` — this is the "done"
290	   marker.
291	
292	Multi-step cycles surface the ladder at the top of
293	`notes/todo.md > ## In Progress` as a bullet list with `(done)` /
294	`(current)` markers — see the file's intro paragraph for the
295	format. When starting a new step, the *first* edit is to mark
296	that step `(current)` in `notes/todo.md` — before any code/doc
297	work — so the In Progress view reflects what's actually
298	happening. The flip back to `(done)` is part of the pre-commit
299	checklist (item 6).
300	
301	**Why numeric suffixes (`-0`, `-1`, …) rather than `-devN`:**
302	semver pre-release identifiers may consist of a single numeric
303	component, and they compare numerically per spec. So
304	`X.Y.Z-1 < X.Y.Z-2 < … < X.Y.Z` correctly orders the dev ladder
305	below the done marker. Cargo accepts this form. The `-dev` prefix
306	adds no information the git log doesn't already convey and
307	doubles typing per commit.

file.content:
### Versioning

Every plan must start with a version bump. Choose the approach based
on scope:

- **Single-step** (recommended for mechanical/focused changes): bump
  directly to `X.Y.Z`, implement in one commit. Simpler history.
- **Multi-step** (for exploratory/large changes): bump to `X.Y.Z-0`,
  implement across multiple commits incrementing the numeric
  suffix. The final commit drops the suffix.

The plan should recommend one approach and get user approval before
starting.

A plan that picks up a `## Todo` item — or *re-scopes* an
existing `## In Progress` ladder to absorb one — **deletes that
`## Todo` entry in the same commit** (the version-bump / plan
commit). `## In Progress` is the sole record of that work until
close-out, when it moves to `## Done`; a cycle that merges
several entangled `## Todo` items deletes all of them. A `## Todo`
entry that duplicates current `## In Progress` work is a process
bug.

For multi-step:

1. Bump version to `X.Y.Z-0` with the plan and commit as a chore
   marker.
2. Implement in one or more `X.Y.Z-N` commits (increment N as
   needed).
3. Final commit bumps to `X.Y.Z` (no suffix), updates
   `notes/todo.md` and `notes/chores/chores-*.md` — this is the "done"
   marker.

Multi-step cycles surface the ladder at the top of
`notes/todo.md > ## In Progress` as a bullet list with `(done)` /
`(current)` markers — see the file's intro paragraph for the
format. When starting a new step, the *first* edit is to mark
that step `(current)` in `notes/todo.md` — before any code/doc
work — so the In Progress view reflects what's actually
happening. The flip back to `(done)` is part of the pre-commit
checklist (item 6).

**Why numeric suffixes (`-0`, `-1`, …) rather than `-devN`:**
semver pre-release identifiers may consist of a single numeric
component, and they compare numerically per spec. So
`X.Y.Z-1 < X.Y.Z-2 < … < X.Y.Z` correctly orders the dev ladder
below the done marker. Cargo accepts this form. The `-dev` prefix
adds no information the git log doesn't already convey and
doubles typing per commit.

The final release commit (no suffix) signals completion rather than

content.content:
424	newest section is briefly `Commits:`-less; that's fine — the
425	commit itself is the record, and `git log --grep "(X.Y.Z)"`
426	finds it.
427	
428	### Pre-commit checklist
429	
430	At cycle *start* (before the version bump): (a) backfill the
431	previous chores section's `Commits:` ref with the just-pushed
432	commit's URL + full SHA — see
433	[Chores commit references](#chores-commit-references); (b) if
434	this cycle picks up a `## Todo` item (or re-scopes `## In
435	Progress` to absorb one), delete that `## Todo` entry — see
436	[Versioning](#versioning).
437	
438	Before proposing a commit, run all of the following and fix any issues:
439	
440	1. `cargo fmt`

file.content:
newest section is briefly `Commits:`-less; that's fine — the
commit itself is the record, and `git log --grep "(X.Y.Z)"`
finds it.

### Pre-commit checklist

At cycle *start* (before the version bump): (a) backfill the
previous chores section's `Commits:` ref with the just-pushed
commit's URL + full SHA — see
[Chores commit references](#chores-commit-references); (b) if
this cycle picks up a `## Todo` item (or re-scopes `## In
Progress` to absorb one), delete that `## Todo` entry — see
[Versioning](#versioning).

Before proposing a commit, run all of the following and fix any issues:

1. `cargo fmt`
2. `cargo clippy`
3. `cargo test`
4. `cargo install --path . --locked` (if applicable) — `--locked`
   is required: without it, `cargo install` ignores `Cargo.lock`
   and re-resolves from scratch, which can pick incompatible
   versions even when `cargo build` / `cargo test` succeed.
5. Retest after install
6. Update `notes/todo.md` — for multi-step cycles, flip the
   just-completed step's marker from `(current)` to `(done)`
   **before** running `vc-x1 push`. The commit being pushed
   should reflect the new state. (The next step's `(current)`
   marker is set later, at the *start* of that step — see the
   Versioning multi-step section.)
7. Update `notes/todo.md` — at cycle close-out (final commit),
   move the entry from `## In Progress` to `## Done`.
8. Update `notes/chores/chores-*.md` — add a section (header =
   provisional commit title; intro paragraph + any `###` design
   subsections; **no** per-file edit list — that's the commit
   body).
9. Sync the chores section header to the **final** commit title
   and update every markdown anchor back-reference to it.
10. Update `notes/README.md` — if functionality changed (new
    flags, new subcommands, changed behavior).

## Sub-step Workflow

input.old_string:
A plan that picks up a `## Todo` item — or *re-scopes* an
existing `## In Progress` ladder to absorb one — **deletes that
`## Todo` entry in the same commit** (the version-bump / plan
commit). `## In Progress` is the sole record of that work until
close-out, when it moves to `## Done`; a cycle that merges
several entangled `## Todo` items deletes all of them. A `## Todo`
entry that duplicates current `## In Progress` work is a process
bug.
input.new_string:
A plan that picks up a `## Todo` item — or *re-scopes* an
existing `## In Progress` ladder to absorb one — **keeps that
`## Todo` entry**: it stays in `## Todo` under a
`### Current In Progress` heading placed immediately above it,
and the `## In Progress` ladder links to it via
`[Current in progress](#current-in-progress)` (the heading
text is fixed, so that anchor is stable across cycles). The
`## Todo` entry carries the task description; `## In Progress`
carries only the ladder — neither duplicates the other. A
cycle that absorbs several entangled `## Todo` items groups
them all under the heading. At close-out the entry moves to
`## Done` and the `### Current In Progress` heading is
removed.

feat: open validate-todo + fix-todo (0.55.0-0)

```
Opens the multi-step 0.55.0 cycle: a `validate-todo` /
`fix-todo` subcommand pair (a Rust replacement for

anchor.

content.text:
**0.55.0-1, `src/todo_helpers.rs` created** — the shared core:

- `Section` enum (`Todo` / `Bugs`), `Change` (per-entry: old/new number, old/new continuation-indent base), `Analysis` (entry counts + changes).
- `analyze(content)` — single-pass walk: tracks the `## ` section, accumulates each entry's continuation block, and emits a `Change` for any entry whose number or measured-base indent is off. Hand-rolled `^\d+\. ` prefix parse, no `regex` dep.
- Headings (`#`/`###`) end an entry's continuation block — so `### Current In Progress` inside `## Todo` is handled cleanly. `## Done` / `## In Progress` / `# References` are skipped.
- Continuation indent measured against each entry's *minimum* non-blank indent (nested `- ` sub-bullets preserved).
- 9 unit tests: clean file, gap renumber, prefix-width indent shift, indent-only issue, nested sub-bullets, independent `## Bugs` numbering, `### ` heading ignored, `## Done` not scanned, blank continuation lines.

Next: `src/validate_todo.rs` + `main.rs` wiring. Review this first?

- `src/validate_todo.rs` — `ValidateTodoArgs` (`[FILE]` positional, defaults to `notes/todo.md`) / `ValidateTodoParams` / `From` / `SubcommandRunner`, all in the standard `(ctx, params)` shape. `validate_todo()` reads the file, runs `todo_helpers::analyze`, prints per-section issue lines (`line N: number X → Y, indent A → B — <snippet>`), and a summary; exits non-zero if any entry needs fixing. `snippet()` truncates first-lines on a `char` boundary; 2 unit tests for it.
- `main.rs` — `mod todo_helpers;` + `mod validate_todo;`; `ValidateTodo` variant (with `long_about`) in `Commands` after `FixDesc`; dispatch arm.

That completes 0.55.0-1's code. On approval I'll run the cargo cycle (fmt / clippy / test / install) and then propose the commit.

- Abandon: revert the cycle to a no-op with an `### Outcome`

## feat: add validate-todo subcommand (0.55.0-1)

First step of the cycle: `validate-todo` and its shared
core `todo_helpers`. `fix-todo` (0.55.0-2) reuses the same
core.

- `todo_helpers::analyze` is the single-pass scanner — it
  walks the file once, tracks the current `## ` section and
  the entry being accumulated, and returns one `Change` per
  entry whose number or continuation indent is off. The
  `^\d+\. ` entry test is hand-rolled (no `regex` dep).
- `validate-todo [FILE]` (FILE defaults to `notes/todo.md`)
  is read-only: it prints each off entry as
  `line N: number X → Y, indent A → B — <snippet>` and
  exits non-zero if any are found. The check half of the
  pair; `fix-todo` is the rewrite.
- Continuation indent is measured against each entry's
  minimum non-blank indent, so a deliberately nested `- `
  sub-bullet (e.g. under `## Bugs` entry 1) is not
  mistaken for a misindent.

Docs (README / ARCHITECTURE) for both subcommands land
together in the 0.55.0-2 sweep.

# References

lastPrompt: I added some comments to the code as I was reviewing, review my changes

content.text:
Reviewed the inline comments you added to `analyze`. Findings:

**Typos — should fix:**
- L158 `number it's entries` → `its` (possessive, not "it is").
- L210 `finsh` → `finish`.

**Accuracy:**
- L176 `// We're not processing any section yet` — "yet" is misleading: `section` is `None` not just *before* `## Todo`/`## Bugs` but also *after* them (inside `## In Progress`, `## Done`, `# References`). Better: `// Not inside a ## Todo / ## Bugs section`.
- L210 `…any last pending line` — it's a pending **entry** (`PendingEntry`), not a line.
- L160 `// Flush any pending entries` — `pending` is `Option<PendingEntry>`, so at most one — "entry" singular.

**Convention:** CLAUDE.md's Code Conventions say inline `//` comments inside function bodies are "discouraged unless they capture a non-obvious WHY." A few of these narrate WHAT the code already states plainly — `// Flush any pending entries`, `// Update information for this entry`, `// Return the Analysis`. By the project's own rule those would be dropped; the WHY-bearing ones (heading-ends-continuation, the continuation-line note) are the kind it keeps. The `analyze` state machine isn't trivial, though, so light signposting at the three branch points (new section / other heading / entry vs continuation) is a defensible exception if you want it.

How do you want to land this:
1. Fix typos + accuracy wording, **trim** the pure-WHAT comments (strict CLAUDE.md).
2. Fix typos + accuracy wording, **keep** the signposting comments as-is otherwise.

Either way I'll fix the typos. Which do you want — trim or keep?

message.content: Trim the pure-WHAT comments
Rereview

content.text:
Re-reviewed. Your fixes landed:

- L158 `it's` → `its` ✓
- L160 → "entry" singular ✓
- L176 dropped the misleading "yet" ✓
- L210 / L215 — the `finsh`-typo'd and `// Return the Analysis` WHAT-comments removed ✓

One new typo introduced at **L182**:

```
// Flush any previous entry we we are working on
```

Doubled "we". Suggest `// Flush any previous entry we were working on` — "were" is also more accurate than "we are" (it's the entry we *were* on, now being finalized as a new one starts).

Nothing else — the remaining signpost comments are consistent and accurate. Want me to fix L182, or will you?

message.content: Fixed, check me again, please

content.text:
**0.55.0-2, group 2** — `src/todo_helpers.rs` extended with the rewrite path:

- `analyze` now also produces **`Analysis.fixed`** — the file content with every `## Todo` / `## Bugs` entry renumbered and re-indented. It round-trips an already-correct file byte-for-byte (switched to `split('\n')` so a trailing newline survives).
- `Change` gains **`new_first_line`** — the entry's first line with the corrected number (for `fix-todo`'s dry-run output).
- The walk now accumulates each entry's raw continuation lines and emits a corrected block on finalize: the block shifts as a whole by `prefix_width − measured_base`, so nested sub-bullets keep their depth.
- `parse_entry_prefix` returns `(number, prefix_len)`; added `leading_spaces` helper.

Heads-up: I **rewrote the `analyze` body** (it now builds output, not just measures), so the inline comments you'd reviewed are redone — accurate signposts at the same branch points. Tests: 14 (the 9 from -1 kept, now also asserting `fixed`; +`nested_block_shifts_preserving_depth`, `fixed_is_idempotent`).

`validate_todo.rs` is unchanged — it still calls `analyze` and ignores the new fields.

Next (group 3): `src/fix_todo.rs` + `main.rs` wiring. Review group 2 first?

content.text:
Ran it. Here's what's happening:

**Diagnosis.** Your test inserted a bare `0` at column 0 *inside* entry #1's continuation block:
```
   Renumber `## Todo` and `## Bugs` entries in `notes/todo.md`,
0
   normalize continuation-line indent to match prefix width.
```
The parser checks `0` for an entry prefix — `^\d+\. ` — and it fails (no `. ` after the `0`), and it's not a heading. So `0` falls through to "continuation line of entry #1." Its indent is 0. The measured base is the **minimum** non-blank continuation indent → `0` drags the base from 3 down to **0**. So `fix-todo` computes `delta = prefix_width − base = 3 − 0 = +3` and shifts the *whole* block: the real continuations 3→6, the `0` itself 0→3. Hence `[indent 0 → 3]` and the over-indented "odd" source. Markdown still renders it because it's tolerant of over-indented list continuations.

**Root cause.** A real continuation line *must* be indented — a column-0 line can't be a list continuation. The measured-base algorithm assumes no continuation line is shallower than the true base; a stray column-0 line breaks that. Your `0` is malformed input — a column-0 line in `## Todo` that's neither `N. ` nor a heading. (It's a test artifact — it has to come out of `todo.md` before the `-2` commit regardless.)

**Options:**

1. **Just remove the test `0`** — no code change. The algorithm stays naive about malformed input.
2. **Harden (small, recommended)** — in `finish_entry`, exclude column-0 lines from the base measurement and emit them verbatim. A continuation is by definition indented, so a col-0 line shouldn't define the base. Result: base stays 3, no false `[indent 0 → 3]`, real continuations untouched, the stray line passes through unchanged. ~2 lines.
3. **Harden + flag** — option 2, plus `validate-todo`/`fix-todo` *report* a column-0 non-entry line as malformed input. A new diagnostic category — bigger; better as a follow-up todo than `-2` scope.

I'd do **2** for this cycle (and note "flag malformed lines" as a future todo). It makes the tool robust against garbage without corrupting good content, and doesn't balloon `-2`.

(Minor, separate: the summary reads "1 entries" — I'll pluralize that while I'm in there.)

Which option do you want?

file.content:
44. Test-tempdir override resolution chain. Both
    `src/test_helpers::unique_base` and
    `tests/common/unique_base` currently use
    `std::env::temp_dir()` (= `$TMPDIR`). Generalize to
    resolve in priority order: explicit env var (e.g.
    `VC_X1_TEST_TMPDIR`) → user config
    (`~/.config/vc-x1/config.toml`) → local
    `.vc-config.toml` → `std::env::temp_dir()` fallback.
    Useful when a developer wants tests on a tmpfs / SSD /
    project-local path without exporting `TMPDIR` globally.
    Open question: do we also expose a CLI parameter
    (e.g. `vc-x1 --workspace-tmp …`)? Test binaries can't
    easily accept arbitrary flags via `cargo test --`, so
    env is the realistic surface for tests; for the
    `vc-x1` binary itself a flag is feasible but unclear
    it adds value over the resolution chain.

## Bugs

content.text:
Agreed — `validate-todo`'s `number 2 → 1 — ` prefix is redundant: the corrected line plus a compact tag already say it. Making it match `fix-todo` is the right call (and you noted early on validate is essentially `fix-todo --dry-run`).

Proposal:

- **Identical per-change line in both:** `  line N: {corrected line}  [tag]`. So `validate-todo` drops `number X → Y — <truncated original>` and shows the corrected line + `[was N]` / `[indent A → B]` tag, exactly like `fix-todo`.
- **Share `change_tag`** — move it from `fix_todo.rs` to `todo_helpers` (pub); both subcommands call it.
- `validate-todo` shows the full corrected line, so its `snippet()` truncation helper goes away.
- `Change.first_line` (the original line) then has no remaining reader — **drop it** from the struct.

Result — `validate-todo`:
```
validate-todo: notes/todo.md

## Todo
  line 45: 1. **single-field `options_flags` leaves → `value` field.**  [was 2]
  line 57: 2. **Investigate `linkme` for subcommand registration.**  [was 3]

46 entries checked (45 Todo, 1 Bugs) — 2 to fix
```

The two commands then differ only by header, summary line, and exit code (validate non-zero). Net change also *removes* code (`snippet` + the `first_line` field). Go?

message.content: Could validate-todo "easily" be made to just invoke `fix-todo --dry-run` so save some lines of code?

content.text:
Short answer: not cleanly — the "easily" instinct is right to be in quotes.

**The blocker is the exit code.** `fix-todo --dry-run` returns success (exit 0) — it's a preview that succeeded. `validate-todo` must exit **non-zero** when the file needs fixing — that's its whole reason to exist as a separate command (the CI gate: `vc-x1 validate-todo && …`). If `validate_todo()` just called `fix_todo()`, it'd always get `Ok(())` back and couldn't gate. You'd have to change `fix_todo`'s return to report "had changes" — at which point validate isn't "just invoking" it, and the *checker* now depends on the *rewriter* module, which is backwards (a read-only check shouldn't pull in the thing that writes files).

**And the duplication is already small.** The genuinely shared *logic* — `analyze`, `change_tag`, `entry_word` — is in `todo_helpers`. What's left duplicated between `validate_todo()` and `fix_todo()` is ~10 lines of thin orchestration: read the file, call `analyze`, and the 8-line per-change print loop. Each subcommand's own part (summary wording, exit code, the write) genuinely differs.

The only non-trivial duplicated bit is that print loop. It *could* move into a shared function — but the only sensible home is `todo_helpers`, and that would make the pure parsing core do `info!` output. `todo_helpers` today is clean: it parses and returns data; `change_tag`/`entry_word` return strings, they don't print. Putting `info!` in there to save 8 lines is a worse trade than the 8 lines.

This mirrors `validate-desc` / `fix-desc` — separate, self-contained subcommands that share `desc_helpers` for logic and don't call each other.

My recommendation: **leave it.** The shared logic is already factored; the remaining duplication is thin and the cleanest, and shrinking it would either make the checker depend on the rewriter or make `todo_helpers` impure. Not worth it for ~10 lines.

Want to proceed to group 4 (delete `fix-todo.py` + docs sweep)?

toolUseResult.originalFile:
# Notes

This directory contains various notes and documentation related to the project.
Each file is organized by topic for easy reference.

By default there are chores-*.md and todo.md. Chores are general notes
about tasks and todo.md contains short term tasks and their status.
The chores-NN files are numbered in sequence; older ones are closed
(chores-09.md and earlier — through the 0.48.x cycles), and the
active one ([chores-10.md](chores-10.md), the 0.49.0 cycle onward)
also carries the **refactor-tracking** tables (the Context+Params
port and the options_flags extraction).

App architecture — the CLI-args / `Context`+`Params` split, a
generic module map, the subcommand model, and what the
Context+Params port and the options_flags extraction *are* (the
*live status* is in chores-10.md) — lives at the repo root in
[`../ARCHITECTURE.md`](../ARCHITECTURE.md).

In the future we I expect we may want to create a "notes"
database to better manage the information, TBD.

Examples chore file:
```
# Chores-01.md
 
General maintenance tasks and considerations for the project see other files for
more specific topics. A chore in a chores file provides quick information on the
how and why of a particular chore.

## Create a binary that lists jj info 

This binary should list the changeID, commitID, and description title
and using `jj-lib`
```

## Workflow and conventions

Bot-facing workflow, versioning, and code conventions live in
[`../CLAUDE.md`](../CLAUDE.md). Start there for:

- **Versioning during development** — single-step vs multi-step,
  `-N` pre-release suffixes, done-marker discipline.
- **Code Conventions** — doc comments on every file / fn / method,
  `// OK: …` justifications on `unwrap*` calls, ask-on-ambiguity,
  stuck detection.
- **Commit-Push-Finalize Flow** — two-checkpoint per-step
  discipline with hard stop after finalize.

## Todo format

Todo.md contains two main sections "Todo" and "Done" each item is a
short explanations of a tasks and links to more details using 1 or more
references.

Todo items use lazy numbering — every entry begins with `1. ` and the
markdown renderer auto-numbers them. Reorder or insert items without
renumbering, and reference an entry by its displayed number ("let's
work on #3"). The Done section keeps `-` bullets — items aren't
referenced by number once completed.

Reference citations are **double-bracketed** — `[[1]]`, or
`[[2]],[[3]]` for several — so the brackets show when rendered
(a bare `[1]` renders as just `1`, which doesn't read as a
reference). Separate multiple with a comma: `[[2]],[[3]]`, not
`[2,3]` (a single ref key — won't resolve) or `[[2]][[3]]`. The
`[N]:` definition lines and inline `[text](url)` / `[text](#anchor)`
links stay single-bracketed (their brackets already show in
source, and the link text reads as a link when rendered).

Examples:

# Todo
- Add new feature X [details](features.md#feature-x)
- Fix bug Y [[1]]

# Done
- Fixed issue Z [[2]],[[3]]

[1]: bugs.md#bug-y
[2]: issues.md#issue-z
[3]: fixes.md#fix-z

## Reference numbering

Every note file (`todo.md`, `chores-NN.md`, `done.md`) keeps a
file-local `# References` section at the bottom. Reference numbers
are scoped to that file — `[1]` in `chores-07.md` and `[1]` in
`chores-01.md` are independent slots that may point at completely
different URLs. New chores files start their numbering at `[1]`.

Treat `[N]` like a **footnote**: the number is a local slot, only
meaningful within its file's `# References`. So a `[N]` *citation*
(bare `[N]`, or doubled `[[N]]` — see below) never reuses another
file's number; to cite a target a sibling file references, pick
your own next-local slot and define it (the same target may carry
a different number in each file). A `[N]` *inside a code span*
(`` `[72]` ``) is different — that's a quoted identifier: literal
text naming a ref-key (often from another file's namespace), data,
not a citation; it needs no definition here. To point at a section
of another file from prose, use an inline link with an anchor —
`[that section](../chores-07.md#…)` — not a bare number.

A `chores-NN.md` `# References` entry is usually a
`/notes/<file>.md#anchor` (or `/ARCHITECTURE.md`) path, but may
also be a **commit reference** —
`[N]: <commit-url-with-12-hex-SHA> "<full-40-hex-SHA>"`, cited by
a section's `Commits:` line. See
[`../CLAUDE.md`](../CLAUDE.md#chores-commit-references) for the
why and the exact shape.

A file's `# References` can be **re-packed** to a contiguous
`[1]..[N]` in first-citation-appearance order — walk the file's
prose in document order (`todo.md`: `## Todo` then `## Done`;
`chores-NN.md`: top to bottom) and number refs as their first
`[[N]]` citation appears. This is a file-local rewrite — only
that file's `[[N]]` citations and `[N]:` definitions move; every
target and sibling file is untouched. A `[[N]]` inside a `` ` ``
code span is a literal token, not a citation, and is left alone.
Do it opportunistically (when the namespace has drifted enough to
annoy), not on a schedule: `todo.md` fragments fastest (entries
land and get pruned every cycle) and is the usual candidate;
`chores-NN.md` / `done.md` are append-mostly and only need it
after an unusual event (e.g. a bulk retrofit that allocated slots
out of document order).

## Retiring Done entries

`todo.md`'s `## Done` section is a rolling buffer of recently shipped
work, not a permanent log. Move entries into `done.md` at two natural
beats:

- **Closing a ladder** — when the final `X.Y.Z` (no suffix) commit
  ships, decide which prior entries are no longer needed for nearby
  context and migrate them.
- **Opening a new ladder** — at `X.Y.Z-0`, do the same sweep before
  bumping the version.

Migration mechanics:

- Move the bullet itself from `todo.md > ## Done` to
  `done.md` (preserving the original ref number).
- Copy any references the moved entries cite into
  `done.md`'s `# References` section (those refs are file-local,
  so coexisting with `todo.md`'s namespace is fine).
- Prune any references in `todo.md > # References` no longer cited
  by anything in `## In Progress` / `## Todo` / `## Done`. This
  frees the numbers for future reuse.

content.text:
Your experiment is correct. Two separate limits are in play:

- **The parser** (`parse_entry_prefix`) skips any line that doesn't start with a digit at column 0 — so **≥ 1 leading space** is all it takes to dodge being read as an entry.
- **The renderer** (CommonMark) strips up to 3 leading spaces before a paragraph — so **1, 2, or 3** spaces all render flush-left, identically. (4+ would turn the first line into an indented code block.)

So "exactly 1" is **not a functional requirement** — it's a *style choice*. And a convention's whole job is to pick one, so the raw source has a single uniform left edge. Your experiment lines are the proof of what relaxing it costs — `## Todo`'s intro is now ragged at 3/3/2/1 spaces; renders fine, reads messy in source.

My recommendation: **keep "exactly 1"**, but make the convention text honest about *why* it's 1 rather than stating it as if it were magic. Proposed rewrite of the `todo.md` intro:

```
Intro paragraphs in `## Todo` and `## Bugs` below begin every
line with exactly 1 leading space so they don't match the
`^\d+\. ` entry pattern `vc-x1 validate-todo` / `fix-todo` use
to find numbered entries. Any indent ≥ 1 dodges that pattern,
and CommonMark strips up to 3 leading spaces before a
paragraph — so 1 to 3 render identically; the convention
fixes it at 1, the minimum, to keep the raw source uniform.
```

And I'd normalize your experiment's ragged `## Todo` intro lines (32–35) back to 1 space each.

Two notes: the tooling deliberately leaves intro lines alone (they're not entries — `fix-todo` passes them through), so it won't catch raggedness; that stays a documented convention, not an enforced one. Go with the rewrite + normalize?

lastPrompt: You say intro paragraphs being with "exactly 1 leading space" but an experiment indicates it renders the same with 1 to 3 spaces?

message.content: I think a little simpler maybe: "Intro paragraphs in `## Todo` and `## Bugs` should begin with 1 leading space so as not to match the regx for locating numbered entries, but 2 or 3 also work.", thoughts?

content.text:
Simpler is good. Two tweaks to your sentence:

- **"begin every line"** — keep "every line": a paragraph is multiple lines, and any line starting `\d. ` at column 0 (not just the first) would be misread as an entry.
- **"regx"** — typo, and since `fix-todo.py` is gone the matcher is now hand-rolled Rust, not an actual regex. Better to name the pattern directly.

Proposed:

```
Intro paragraphs in `## Todo` and `## Bugs` should begin every
line with 1 leading space so they don't match the `^\d+\. `
pattern that locates numbered entries; 2 or 3 spaces also work.
```

One consequence to be aware of: this wording **relaxes** the old "exactly 1" — it now says 1 is preferred but 2–3 are fine. So your experiment's ragged `## Todo` intro (lines 32–35, at 3/3/2/1) technically conforms now. I'd still tidy those back to 1 space so the section reads uniform in source — but with this wording it's optional, your call.

Go with this sentence? And tidy the experiment lines, or leave them?

file.content:
# vc-x1

- [Overview](#vc-x1)
- [Architecture](ARCHITECTURE.md)
- [Usage](#usage)
  - [Revision shortcuts](#revision-shortcuts)
  - [Shell completion](#shell-completion)
  - [validate-desc](#validate-desc)
  - [fix-desc](#fix-desc)
  - [clone](#clone)
  - [init](#init)
  - [symlink](#symlink)
  - [finalize](#finalize)
  - [Testing push + finalize](#testing-push--finalize)
- [Cross-repo Linking with Git Trailers](#cross-repo-linking-with-git-trailers)
- [jj Tips for Git Users](#jj-tips-for-git-users)
- [Thoughts for the future](#thoughts-for-the-future)
- [Contributing](#contributing)
- [License](#license)

This is experiment 1 to explore creating a Vibe Coding (vc) environment.
We will investigate ways of using the dual jj-git repo concept, explored
in [hw-jjg-bot](https://github.com/winksaville/hw-jjg-bot.git) to
initially make it easy to see how the code base evolved. This is
made possible by the fact that we have two repos one with the code
and one with the conversation with the bot.

I've chosen the jj-git environment because jj provides the concept that
each commit has an immutable changeID as well as the mutable commitID
of git. The idea is that each commit made on repo A writes the
changeID in the commit message to repo B. Thus there is a cross reference
between the two repos and this will allow vc-x1 to show how the repo
evolved and the entity (bot or human) can more clearly understand **how** and
most importantly **why** the code evolved.

The solution space is wide open, from trivial CLI, web or app based
(mobile/non-mobile). In addition, I could see this as an extension to
existing programming editors like vscode and zed or even creating our
own IDE for vc.

See [Initial commit with dual jj-git repos](./notes/chores/chores-01.md#initial-commit-with-dual-jj-git-repos)
for how the initial commit was created with the dual jj-git repos. After
doing so and I then created this README.md file.

## Usage

```
vc-x1 list [-r REVISION] [-n COMMITS]  # List commits in a jj repo
vc-x1 desc [-r REVISION] [-n COMMITS]  # Show full description of a commit
vc-x1 chid [-r REVISION] [-n COMMITS]  # Print changeID(s) for a revision
vc-x1 show [-r REVISION] [-n COMMITS]  # Show commit details and diff summary
vc-x1 validate-desc [OPTS]                 # Validate commit descriptions
vc-x1 fix-desc [OPTS]                     # Fix commit descriptions (dry-run default)
vc-x1 clone <REPO> [NAME] [OPTS]          # Clone a dual-repo project
vc-x1 init <NAME> [OPTS]                  # Create a new dual-repo project
vc-x1 symlink [TARGET] [OPTS]             # Create Claude Code project symlink
vc-x1 sync [OPTS]                          # Fetch + sync both repos to their remotes
vc-x1 finalize --bookmark <B> [OPTS]       # Squash working copy into target
vc-x1 --version                            # Print version
vc-x1 --help                           # Print help
```

content.content:
242	### fix-desc
243	
244	Fix commit descriptions against the other repo. Default is dry-run —
245	use `--no-dry-run` to write changes. Reads other repo from
246	`.vc-config.toml` by default.
247	
248	```
249	# Dry-run: show what would be fixed
250	vc-x1 fix-desc @..
251	
252	# Actually fix
253	vc-x1 fix-desc @.. --no-dry-run
254	
255	# Add missing ochid trailers by matching title
256	vc-x1 fix-desc @.. --add-missing
257	
258	# Limit fixes
259	vc-x1 fix-desc @.. --no-dry-run -m 3
260	
261	# Use a fallback for IDs not found in other repo
262	vc-x1 fix-desc @.. --fallback /.claude/lost
263	```
264	
265	| Flag | Description |
266	|------|-------------|
267	| `--other-repo <PATH>` | Override other repo from .vc-config.toml |
268	| `--no-dry-run` | Write fixes [default: dry-run] |
269	| `--add-missing` | Infer and add ochid for commits without one |
270	| `-m, --max-fixes <N>` | Stop fixing after N commits changed [default: all] |
271	| `--fallback <VALUE>` | Replacement for IDs not found in other repo |
272	| `--id-len <N>` | Expected changeID length [default: 12] |
273	| `--title <TEXT>` | Replace commit title at the same time |
274	
275	Use `--help` for the full status label legend.
276	
277	### clone
278	
279	Clone an existing dual-repo project. Runs `git clone --recursive` to
280	get both repos, initializes `jj` in each, and creates the Claude Code
281	symlink.
282	
283	```

file.content:
### fix-desc

Fix commit descriptions against the other repo. Default is dry-run —
use `--no-dry-run` to write changes. Reads other repo from
`.vc-config.toml` by default.

```
# Dry-run: show what would be fixed
vc-x1 fix-desc @..

# Actually fix
vc-x1 fix-desc @.. --no-dry-run

# Add missing ochid trailers by matching title
vc-x1 fix-desc @.. --add-missing

# Limit fixes
vc-x1 fix-desc @.. --no-dry-run -m 3

# Use a fallback for IDs not found in other repo
vc-x1 fix-desc @.. --fallback /.claude/lost
```

| Flag | Description |
|------|-------------|
| `--other-repo <PATH>` | Override other repo from .vc-config.toml |
| `--no-dry-run` | Write fixes [default: dry-run] |
| `--add-missing` | Infer and add ochid for commits without one |
| `-m, --max-fixes <N>` | Stop fixing after N commits changed [default: all] |
| `--fallback <VALUE>` | Replacement for IDs not found in other repo |
| `--id-len <N>` | Expected changeID length [default: 12] |
| `--title <TEXT>` | Replace commit title at the same time |

Use `--help` for the full status label legend.

### clone

Clone an existing dual-repo project. Runs `git clone --recursive` to
get both repos, initializes `jj` in each, and creates the Claude Code
symlink.

```

input.new_string:
Use `--help` for the full status label legend.

### validate-todo

Check that the `## Todo` and `## Bugs` sections of a todo file
are numbered `1..N` in document order, with continuation-line
indent matching each entry's number-prefix width. Read-only;
exits non-zero if any entry needs fixing.

```
# Check notes/todo.md (the default)
vc-x1 validate-todo

# Check a specific file
vc-x1 validate-todo path/to/todo.md
```

Each flagged entry prints its corrected first line and a
`[line: …]` tag — the entry's line number and what is off
(`was N`, `indent A → B`). Use `fix-todo` to apply the fixes.

### fix-todo

Renumber a todo file's `## Todo` and `## Bugs` sections to
`1..N` and normalize each entry's continuation-line indent.
Dry-run by default — prints each changed entry's corrected
line so the output is the result; `--no-dry-run` writes the
file in place. Replaces the interim `notes/fix-todo.py`.

```
# Dry-run: print the corrected lines
vc-x1 fix-todo

# Renumber the file in place
vc-x1 fix-todo --no-dry-run

# Operate on a specific file
vc-x1 fix-todo path/to/todo.md
```

| Flag | Description |
|------|-------------|
| `--no-dry-run` | Write the renumbered file in place [default: dry-run] |

### clone

# Architecture

How the `vc-x1` binary is structured: the clap-aware CLI layer
and the (emerging) clap-free subcommand layer. This is the
"what + some why" — generic and slow-moving. The *how* and the
per-cycle / per-subcommand status live in `notes/chores/chores-*.md`
and `notes/todo.md`.

This is a living document; the app is under continuous
development and the repo will keep changing.

## Overview

`vc-x1` is a single binary that supports subcommands,
`vc-x1 <subcommand>`, for managing a jj-git **dual-repo workspace**
with an app repo plus a `.claude` bot-session repo. The commits
cross-reference each other via `ochid:` trailers at the bottom
of each commit message. See [`README.md`](README.md) for the
user-facing picture and [`CLAUDE.md`](CLAUDE.md) for the bot
workflow.

## Two layers: CLI args vs subcommand Context + Params

Goal: separate clap-aware argument parsing from subcommand
operation logic, so a future front-end (TUI, library embedding)
can call the same core without dragging clap along. The trigger
was `args.account.account`-style nested-leaf access leaking into
subcommand bodies; accessor shortcuts were rejected as hiding
the mismatch rather than fixing it.

- **CLI layer (clap-aware)**: builds `XxxParams` from the
  user-facing `XxxArgs` `#[derive(Args)]` clap struct.
  Options/flags used in more than one subcommand live as
  flattened leaves/bundles in `src/options_flags/`.
- **Subcommand layer (clap-free)**: entrypoint shape
  `fn x(ctx: &Context, params: &XxxParams) -> Result<…>` —
  isolates the subcommand from clap and from the user.
  - `Context` — platform state shared by every subcommand.
  - `XxxParams` — that subcommand's inputs, derived from
    config / command-line args.
- **Boundary conversion**: `XxxArgs → XxxParams` via
  `impl From<&XxxArgs>` or `impl TryFrom<&XxxArgs>`, depending
  on whether the conversion can fail.

Two parameters per subcommand; the signature documents what the
subcommand depends on. `Context` is the same across all
subcommands; `XxxParams` carry the per-subcommand inputs.

Rules for the subcommand layer (some still aspirational — see
[the Context+Params port](#args--context--params)):

1. Plain options, flat fields, `Default` — no clap types in
   `XxxParams`. Domain types (`RepoSelector`, `Scope`) are
   fine; leaf wrappers (`RepoOption`) are not.
2. Typed errors (`enum XError`, not `Box<dyn Error>`) — a TUI
   matches variants to pick dialogs; CLI formats them.
3. Returned outcomes, not `println!` — each subcommand returns
   a structured result; CLI formats it, a library writes
   nothing to stdout itself.
4. Progress via an optional `&mut dyn ProgressSink` on
   long-running subcommands — CLI installs a stderr sink, tests
   a recording sink.
5. No globals, no implicit cwd/env reads in subcommands — CLI
   resolves cwd/env once at startup and builds `Context`.

None of this is sacred — the whole structure is an experiment.
The current scope is deliberately narrow: a small `Context`
(two params, not one god blob); no trait-based DI yet; one
crate, not a workspace split; `src/options_flags/` leaves left
in place — only their consumers change shape. Widen any of it
when the evidence (a second front-end, a second consumer crate,
a real platform-surface divergence) calls for it — not before.

### Naming

Earlier drafts of this design used `Workspace` / `XOptions`;
the implementation (0.44.0) chose `Context` / `XxxParams`:

- `Workspace` → `Context` — Cargo owns "workspace" formally,
  and this codebase already uses "workspace" for the dual-repo
  project root (`find_workspace_root`).
- `XOptions` → `XxxParams` — avoids visual collision with
  `Option<T>` (a params struct mixes required and
  `Option<T>`-typed fields) and with `src/options_flags/`.

## Module map

The *kinds* of module and what they're for — not an exhaustive
catalog (read the `//!` docstrings; `notes/chores/chores-*.md` for
per-subcommand refactor status):

**CLI layer:**

- `src/main.rs` — the clap edge: `Cli` (`Parser`), `Commands`
  (`Subcommand`) + the dispatch arms, `cli_with_banner()` (the
  per-subcommand `vc-x1 X.Y.Z` banner), `CompleteEnv` wiring,
  and `bm_track` (the permanent `main`-bookmark tracking
  diagnostic on every command's entry/exit).
- `src/options_flags/` — reusable `#[derive(Args)]` *leaves*
  (one flag, or a small fixed pair) and *bundles*
  (`#[command(flatten)]` of N leaves) shared across
  subcommands, so a flag is defined (parser, help, completer)
  exactly once. See
  [`src/options_flags/README.md`](src/options_flags/README.md).
- `src/common.rs` — helpers the read-only commit-query
  subcommands (`chid` / `desc` / `list` / `show`) share:
  `for_each_repo`, `collect_ids`, `resolve_revset`, the
  `DotSpec` / `..`-notation parsing, `Header` /
  `resolve_header`, the `format_*` printers, and the
  `-R`/`-s` resolution stack — `find_workspace_root` /
  `default_scope` / `scope_to_repos` / `resolve_repos`
  (the top-level entry combining the `-R PATH` override and
  the `-s code|bot|code,bot` role selection).

**Subcommand-layer scaffolding:**

- `src/context.rs` — `Context`, the shared platform handle
  (today: the loaded `UserConfig` + the `--log` path; built
  once at startup via `Context::load(log)`).
- `src/config.rs` — `UserConfig` (`~/.config/vc-x1/config.toml`).
- `src/options_flags/scope.rs` — `Scope` (a newtype over
  `Vec<Side>` — the `code`/`bot` role set) and `parse_scope`.

**Subcommand modules** — `src/<x>.rs` holds the subcommand's
`XxxArgs` (`#[derive(Args)]`, composing `options_flags/` leaves
where one exists) and the entrypoint `pub fn x(...)`. Modules:
`chid`, `desc` (+ `desc_helpers`), `list`, `show`,
`validate_desc`, `fix_desc`, `clone`, `init` (+ `init/params`),
`symlink`, `sync`, `finalize`, `push` (a resumable state
machine). Which ones have ported to `(ctx, params)` and which
compose `options_flags/` leaves: `notes/chores/chores-*.md` +
`notes/todo.md`.

**Support:**

- `src/repo_utils.rs`, `src/url.rs`, `src/toml_simple.rs`,
  `src/logging.rs` — jj/repo helpers, URL parsing, a minimal
  TOML reader, the CLI logger.
- `src/test_helpers.rs`, `src/test_tmp_root.rs` —
  `#[cfg(test)]` fixtures and tempdir resolution. Subcommand
  modules also carry their own `#[cfg(test)] mod tests` (and
  `integration_tests` for `sync` / `push`).

## Subcommand model

Adding a subcommand `x`:

1. Create `src/x.rs` with `pub struct XxxArgs`
   (`#[derive(Args)]`), composing leaves from
   `src/options_flags/` where one exists.
2. Add a variant `X(x::XxxArgs)` to `Commands` in `src/main.rs`
   with a `///` summary (and `#[command(long_about = …)]` for
   the long form).
3. Add a dispatch arm in `main()` calling `x::x(&args)` (or,
   once ported, `x::x(&ctx, &params)`).
4. Doc comments on `#[arg(...)]` fields drive `--help`; add
   `#[arg(verbatim_doc_comment, …)]` on any field whose doc
   comment uses bullets (clap otherwise reflows them into
   prose).
5. Dynamic value completion (`--account=<TAB>`) is an
   `ArgValueCompleter` attached to the leaf in
   `src/options_flags/` — completion is a clap-aware concern
   and stays at this layer.

## args → Context + Params

"The Context+Params port" — each subcommand's
`pub fn x(args: &XxxArgs)` is structured as
`pub fn x(ctx: &Context, params: &XxxParams)`, with an
`XxxParams` flat struct + a `From` (or `TryFrom`, if the
conversion is fallible) at the binary edge. `init` (0.44.0)
is the worked example; complete across all 12 current
subcommands as of 0.49.0, and the shape new subcommands
follow. Out of scope until a real consumer surfaces: typed
errors, returned-outcomes-vs-`println!`, `ProgressSink`,
`Context` fields beyond `UserConfig` + `--log`. Per-subcommand
status: `notes/chores/chores-*.md` (the "Refactor tracking" section).

## per-subcommand flags → `src/options_flags/`

Independently of the Context+Params port — "the options_flags
extraction" — per-subcommand inline `#[arg]` fields are being
lifted into reusable leaves/bundles under `src/options_flags/`
so a flag is defined (parser, help, completer) exactly once. See
[`src/options_flags/README.md`](src/options_flags/README.md)
for the leaf / bundle / Pattern-A mechanics and the
Flag-vs-Option classification; `notes/chores/chores-*.md` for
per-subcommand status.

## See also

- [`README.md`](README.md) — user-facing overview and
  per-subcommand usage.
- [`CLAUDE.md`](CLAUDE.md) — bot workflow, versioning,
  commit/push conventions, code conventions.
- [`src/options_flags/README.md`](src/options_flags/README.md)
  — leaf/bundle patterns for the options_flags extraction.
- [`notes/todo.md`](notes/todo.md) — live task list, including
  both refactors.
- `notes/chores/chores-*.md` — per-cycle design captures and the
  refactor-tracking tables (currently
  [`chores-10.md`](notes/chores/chores-10.md); `chores-09.md` and
  earlier are closed).

per-subcommand refactor status):

**CLI layer:**

- `src/main.rs` — the clap edge: `Cli` (`Parser`), `Commands`
  (`Subcommand`) + the dispatch arms, `cli_with_banner()` (the
  per-subcommand `vc-x1 X.Y.Z` banner), `CompleteEnv` wiring,
  and `bm_track` (the permanent `main`-bookmark tracking
  diagnostic on every command's entry/exit).
- `src/options_flags/` — reusable `#[derive(Args)]` *leaves*
  (one flag, or a small fixed pair) and *bundles*
  (`#[command(flatten)]` of N leaves) shared across
  subcommands, so a flag is defined (parser, help, completer)
  exactly once. See
  [`src/options_flags/README.md`](src/options_flags/README.md).
- `src/common.rs` — helpers the read-only commit-query
  subcommands (`chid` / `desc` / `list` / `show`) share:
  `for_each_repo`, `collect_ids`, `resolve_revset`, the
  `DotSpec` / `..`-notation parsing, `Header` /
  `resolve_header`, the `format_*` printers, and the
  `-R`/`-s` resolution stack — `find_workspace_root` /
  `default_scope` / `scope_to_repos` / `resolve_repos`
  (the top-level entry combining the `-R PATH` override and
  the `-s code|bot|code,bot` role selection).

**Subcommand-layer scaffolding:**

- `src/context.rs` — `Context`, the shared platform handle
  (today: the loaded `UserConfig` + the `--log` path; built
  once at startup via `Context::load(log)`).
- `src/config.rs` — `UserConfig` (`~/.config/vc-x1/config.toml`).
- `src/options_flags/scope.rs` — `Scope` (a newtype over
  `Vec<Side>` — the `code`/`bot` role set) and `parse_scope`.

**Subcommand modules** — `src/<x>.rs` holds the subcommand's
`XxxArgs` (`#[derive(Args)]`, composing `options_flags/` leaves
where one exists) and the entrypoint `pub fn x(...)`. Modules:
`chid`, `desc` (+ `desc_helpers`), `list`, `show`,
`validate_desc`, `fix_desc`, `validate_todo` / `fix_todo`
(+ `todo_helpers`), `clone`, `init` (+ `init/params`),
`symlink`, `sync`, `finalize`, `push` (a resumable state
machine). Which ones have ported to `(ctx, params)` and which
compose `options_flags/` leaves: `notes/chores/chores-*.md` +
`notes/todo.md`.

**Support:**

- `src/repo_utils.rs`, `src/url.rs`, `src/toml_simple.rs`,
  `src/logging.rs` — jj/repo helpers, URL parsing, a minimal
  TOML reader, the CLI logger.
- `src/test_helpers.rs`, `src/test_tmp_root.rs` —
  `#[cfg(test)]` fixtures and tempdir resolution. Subcommand
  modules also carry their own `#[cfg(test)] mod tests` (and
  `integration_tests` for `sync` / `push`).

## Subcommand model

Adding a subcommand `x`:

1. Create `src/x.rs` with `pub struct XxxArgs`
   (`#[derive(Args)]`), composing leaves from
   `src/options_flags/` where one exists.
2. Add a variant `X(x::XxxArgs)` to `Commands` in `src/main.rs`
   with a `///` summary (and `#[command(long_about = …)]` for
   the long form).
3. Add a dispatch arm in `main()` calling `x::x(&args)` (or,
   once ported, `x::x(&ctx, &params)`).
4. Doc comments on `#[arg(...)]` fields drive `--help`; add
   `#[arg(verbatim_doc_comment, …)]` on any field whose doc
   comment uses bullets (clap otherwise reflows them into
   prose).
5. Dynamic value completion (`--account=<TAB>`) is an
   `ArgValueCompleter` attached to the leaf in
   `src/options_flags/` — completion is a clap-aware concern
   and stays at this layer.

## args → Context + Params

"The Context+Params port" — each subcommand's
`pub fn x(args: &XxxArgs)` is structured as
`pub fn x(ctx: &Context, params: &XxxParams)`, with an
`XxxParams` flat struct + a `From` (or `TryFrom`, if the
conversion is fallible) at the binary edge. `init` (0.44.0)
is the worked example; complete across all 12 current
subcommands as of 0.49.0, and the shape new subcommands
follow. Out of scope until a real consumer surfaces: typed
errors, returned-outcomes-vs-`println!`, `ProgressSink`,
`Context` fields beyond `UserConfig` + `--log`. Per-subcommand
status: `notes/chores/chores-*.md` (the "Refactor tracking" section).

## per-subcommand flags → `src/options_flags/`

Independently of the Context+Params port — "the options_flags
extraction" — per-subcommand inline `#[arg]` fields are being
lifted into reusable leaves/bundles under `src/options_flags/`
so a flag is defined (parser, help, completer) exactly once. See
[`src/options_flags/README.md`](src/options_flags/README.md)
for the leaf / bundle / Pattern-A mechanics and the
Flag-vs-Option classification; `notes/chores/chores-*.md` for
per-subcommand status.

## See also

- [`README.md`](README.md) — user-facing overview and
  per-subcommand usage.
- [`CLAUDE.md`](CLAUDE.md) — bot workflow, versioning,
  commit/push conventions, code conventions.
- [`src/options_flags/README.md`](src/options_flags/README.md)
  — leaf/bundle patterns for the options_flags extraction.
- [`notes/todo.md`](notes/todo.md) — live task list, including
  both refactors.
- `notes/chores/chores-*.md` — per-cycle design captures and the
  refactor-tracking tables (currently
  [`chores-10.md`](notes/chores/chores-10.md); `chores-09.md` and
  earlier are closed).

input.new_string:
Docs (README / ARCHITECTURE) for both subcommands land
together in the 0.55.0-2 sweep.

## feat: add fix-todo subcommand (0.55.0-2)

Second step: `fix-todo`, the rewrite half of the pair, plus
the cycle's docs sweep. `fix-todo` renumbers `## Todo` /
`## Bugs` to `1..N` and normalizes continuation indent —
dry-run by default (printing each changed entry's corrected
line), `--no-dry-run` writes.

- `todo_helpers::analyze` grew to also return `fixed`, the
  fully renumbered / re-indented file content, so `fix-todo`
  writes one assembled result; it round-trips an
  already-correct file byte-for-byte.
- `validate-todo` and `fix-todo` print an identical
  per-entry line, `<corrected line>  [line: …]`. The tag
  builder `change_tag` moved into `todo_helpers` as a shared
  helper; this also de-noised `validate-todo`, which had
  spelled out `number X → Y` against a truncated original.
- `notes/fix-todo.py` deleted — the Rust pair replaces it.
  Docs sweep: root `README.md`, `ARCHITECTURE.md` module
  map, `notes/README.md`'s stale lazy-numbering text, and
  the `notes/todo.md` intro.

### Column-0 lines

`fix-todo` measures an entry's continuation base indent as
the minimum non-blank continuation indent. A line at column
0 — neither an entry (`N. `) nor a heading, so malformed —
would make that minimum 0 and shift the whole block by the
prefix width. A list continuation must be indented, so
column-0 lines are dropped from the measurement and passed
through untouched. Flagging them as malformed input is a
separate follow-up captured in `## Todo`.

# References

**0.55.0-2 is complete.** Group 4 (delete `fix-todo.py` + docs sweep) done; chores `-2` section added; ladder marker flipped.

Proposed commit for **0.55.0-2**:

**Title:**
```

```

**Body:**
```
Second step of the 0.55.0 cycle: `fix-todo`, the rewrite
half of the validate-todo / fix-todo pair, plus the cycle's
docs sweep and the retirement of `notes/fix-todo.py`.
`fix-todo` renumbers `## Todo` / `## Bugs` to 1..N and
normalizes continuation indent — dry-run by default,
`--no-dry-run` writes. `validate-todo` and `fix-todo` now
print an identical per-entry line.

  renumbered content); `change_tag` added as a shared `pub`
  helper; column-0 lines excluded from the indent base;
  `Change.first_line` dropped; `entry_word` helper.

  `fix-todo` via the shared `change_tag`; `snippet` removed.

  arm.
- notes/fix-todo.py: deleted — replaced by the Rust pair.
- README.md: usage entries + `validate-todo` / `fix-todo`
  sections.
- ARCHITECTURE.md: module map gains `validate_todo` /
  `fix_todo` / `todo_helpers`.
- notes/README.md: `## Todo format` rewritten — manual
  numbering (was the stale "lazy numbering").
- notes/todo.md: intro updated (fix-todo.py refs gone); new
  `## Todo` item — flag malformed lines; ladder marker.
- notes/chores/chores-11.md: 0.55.0-2 section; `Commits:
  [[14]]` backfilled on 0.55.0-1; `[14]` ref.
- Cargo.toml / Cargo.lock: 0.55.0-1 → 0.55.0-2.
```

Shall I run `vc-x1 push main`?

content.text:
Before I run the close-out — one thing to confirm, because it has a CLAUDE.md ripple.

`0.55.0-0` rewrote CLAUDE.md's workflow rule from **delete-on-pickup** to the **keep-#1 + `### Current In Progress` + link** model (Versioning section + pre-commit checklist items b and 7). I read your message as: that model was a one-cycle experiment for easy dogfooding, and you want to drop it — not just remove this instance of the link, but abandon the model.

If so, the close-out (`0.55.0`) does:

- **`CLAUDE.md`** — revert the `0.55.0-0` keep-#1 changes back to delete-on-pickup (Versioning paragraph + checklist b/7).
- **`notes/todo.md`**:
  - `## In Progress` — drop the ladder and the `[Current in progress]` link; restore `_No cycle currently in progress._`.
  - `## Todo` — drop the `### Current In Progress` heading; remove item #1 (this cycle's task); renumber the rest via `vc-x1 fix-todo --no-dry-run` (the dogfood).
  - `## Done` — add the cycle entry.
- **`notes/chores/chores-11.md`** — close-out section: as-built ladder + an `### Outcome` recording that the keep-#1 model was tried and reverted; backfill `0.55.0-2`'s `Commits:` ref.
- **`Cargo.toml`** — `0.55.0-2` → `0.55.0`.

The chores opener's `### Keeping the active task in ## Todo` subsection stays as committed history; the close-out `### Outcome` documents the reversal (the opener's `### Per-step evaluation` explicitly allowed an "Abandon" outcome).

Is that the right scope — fully revert the keep-#1 model in CLAUDE.md? Or did you mean only remove the link and leave CLAUDE.md's keep-#1 rule in place?

lastPrompt: close out, but let's remove the "[Current in progress](#current-in-progress)" it's better to move the actual todo I just wanted to do it to allow easy initial dog fooding

message.content: I lean to reverting, but I do like keeping the "current" todo we are working on in todo until we're done, using the link is "easy" but it's probably better moving it from "## Todo" into "## In Progress" and duplicating it in chores and then at the end deleting it from todo. Thoughts
I lean to reverting, and instead codifying moving the "current" Todo into "## In Progress" and duplicating it in chores and then at the end deleting it from todo. Thoughts

content.text:
Agreed on reverting keep-#1. And the move-into-`## In Progress` idea is sound — it fixes the real weakness of the *old* delete-on-pickup model: that one deleted the Todo entry outright, leaving `## In Progress` a cryptic abstract ladder (`0.xx.y-0 blah`). Moving the task text *into* `## In Progress` makes the section self-describing.

Two things to pin down on the model:

- **`## Done`** — you said "delete it from todo" at the end. I'd still add the standard one-line `## Done` entry (the rolling buffer that drains to `done.md`); "delete" = remove the `## In Progress` block, Done still gets its one-liner. Right?
- **"duplicate in chores"** — the chores opener already captures the cycle (design intro + bullets). I'd treat that as the capture, not a verbatim copy of the Todo text — task statement in `## In Progress`, design in chores, cross-linked. Match your intent?

On sequencing, my recommendation: **don't codify the new model inside the `0.55.0` close-out.** The workflow has churned three times this cycle (delete-on-pickup → keep-#1 → move-to-In-Progress); a close-out should be a clean "ship the cycle," not where a fourth-draft workflow gets designed. So:

- **`0.55.0` close-out** — revert `CLAUDE.md` to plain delete-on-pickup (its exact pre-`0.55.0-0` state), normal todo→Done, dogfood the renumber. Ships the subcommands cleanly.
- **`0.56.0`** — a small focused cycle that codifies move-into-`## In Progress`: the CLAUDE.md rewrite plus the `## In Progress` format update, reviewed on its own.

If you'd rather fold it in (revert keep-#1 and go straight to move-to-In-Progress in one close-out — rewrites the Versioning paragraph once instead of twice), I'm fine with that too — I'd just want the two clarifications settled first.

Which way — separate `0.56.0`, or fold into the close-out?

lastPrompt: I lean to reverting, and instead codifying moving the "current" Todo into "## In Progress" and duplicating it in chores and then at the end deleting it from todo. Thoughts

1. `cargo fmt`
2. `cargo clippy`
3. `cargo test`
4. `cargo install --path . --locked` (if applicable) — `--locked`
   is required: without it, `cargo install` ignores `Cargo.lock`

   just-completed step's marker from `(current)` to `(done)`
   **before** running `vc-x1 push`. The commit being pushed
   should reflect the new state. (The next step's `(current)`
   marker is set later, at the *start* of that step — see the
   Versioning multi-step section.)

   `## Todo` into `## Done`, drop that heading, clear the
   `## In Progress` ladder, then renumber `## Todo`.

existing `## In Progress` ladder to absorb one — **keeps that
`## Todo` entry**: it stays in `## Todo` under a

and the `## In Progress` ladder links to it via

text is fixed, so that anchor is stable across cycles). The
`## Todo` entry carries the task description; `## In Progress`

input.new_string:
A plan that picks up a `## Todo` item — or *re-scopes* an
existing `## In Progress` ladder to absorb one — **deletes that
`## Todo` entry in the same commit** (the version-bump / plan
commit). `## In Progress` is the sole record of that work until
close-out, when it moves to `## Done`; a cycle that merges
several entangled `## Todo` items deletes all of them. A `## Todo`
entry that duplicates current `## In Progress` work is a process
bug.

## Todo

 A markdown list of tasks to do in the near future, ordered
 highest-priority first. Keep entries brief — 1-3 lines.
 Detailed motivation, safety requirements, and ordering belong
 in `notes/chores/chores-NN.md` design subsections; link via `[N]` ref.

 Entries are manually numbered (`1.` `2.` …) so the index is
 readable in the raw markdown source. Reorder/insert renumbers the
 entries below — accept the diff churn in exchange for being
 able to count without rendering. Reference by entry number
 ("let's work on #3"). Continuation lines under a two-digit
 entry indent 4 spaces (under `NN. `) rather than 3.

### Current In Progress
1. **`vc-x1 fix-todo` / `validate-todo` subcommands.** Parallel
   shape to `validate-desc` / `fix-desc` (check vs rewrite).
   Renumber `## Todo` and `## Bugs` entries in `notes/todo.md`,
   normalize continuation-line indent to match prefix width.
   Rust replacement for the interim `notes/fix-todo.py`.
   Belongs in the broader "notes/repo hygiene" wave alongside
   `validate-repo` ([[19]]).
2. **single-field `options_flags` leaves → `value` field.**
   `0.47.0` introduced the convention (single-field leaf names
   its field `value`, declares the flag via `#[arg(long = "…")]`,
   so consumers read `args.<leaf>.value` not `args.<leaf>.<leaf>`)
   on the new `squash` leaf. Sweep the pre-existing single-field
   leaves to match: `repo`, `dry_run`, `private`, `account`,
   `config`, `use_template` + their consumers
   (`init.rs`, tests).

1	# Todo
2	
3	This file contains near term tasks with a short description
4	and reference links to more details.
5	
6	Intro paragraphs in `## Todo` and `## Bugs` should begin every
7	line with 1 leading space so they don't match the `^\d+\. `
8	pattern that locates numbered entries; 2 or 3 spaces also work.
9	
10	## In Progress
11	
12	A bulleted list of the in-progress task's development "ladder":
13	   - 0.xx.y-0 blah (done)
14	   - 0.xx.y-1 blah blah (current)
15	   - 0.xx.y-2 blah blah blah
16	   - 0.xx.y close-out and validation
17	
18	_No cycle currently in progress._
19	
20	## Todo
21	
22	 A markdown list of tasks to do in the near future, ordered
23	 highest-priority first. Keep entries brief — 1-3 lines.
24	 Detailed motivation, safety requirements, and ordering belong
25	 in `notes/chores/chores-NN.md` design subsections; link via `[N]` ref.
26	
27	 Entries are manually numbered (`1.` `2.` …) so the index is
28	 readable in the raw markdown source. Reorder/insert renumbers the
29	 entries below — accept the diff churn in exchange for being
30	 able to count without rendering. Reference by entry number
31	 ("let's work on #3"). Continuation lines under a two-digit
32	 entry indent 4 spaces (under `NN. `) rather than 3.
33	
34	1. **single-field `options_flags` leaves → `value` field.**
35	   `0.47.0` introduced the convention (single-field leaf names
36	   its field `value`, declares the flag via `#[arg(long = "…")]`,
37	   so consumers read `args.<leaf>.value` not `args.<leaf>.<leaf>`)
38	   on the new `squash` leaf. Sweep the pre-existing single-field
39	   leaves to match: `repo`, `dry_run`, `private`, `account`,
40	   `config`, `use_template` + their consumers
41	   (`init.rs`, tests).
42	
43	   Note: can a single field be defined as an type or enum instead
44	   of a struct and maybe eliminate the `args.<leaf>.<leaf>` name
45	   issue.
46	2. **Investigate `linkme` for subcommand registration.**
47	   Distributed-slice registry — each subcommand registers itself
48	   at link time; `main.rs` discovers them via the slice rather
49	   than matching a `Commands` enum. Reduces per-subcommand
50	   touchpoints from 3 (mod decl + enum variant + match arm) to 1
51	   (registration). Costs: loses compile-time exhaustiveness
52	   (missing registration = runtime gap); help-output ordering
53	   depends on link order unless sorted; macro-magic dependency.
54	   Revisit once the `0.50.0` trait sweep's per-arm cost has been
55	   felt under real "add a subcommand" load.
56	   <https://github.com/dtolnay/linkme>
57	3. **Investigate `inventory` as `linkme` alternative.** Same
58	   shape as `linkme` — runtime-iterable registry populated by
59	   `inventory::submit!` per subcommand. Trade-offs mirror
60	   linkme's. Pick one if/when the trait sweep's match becomes
61	   the felt bottleneck.
62	   <https://github.com/dtolnay/inventory>
63	4. **`por → dual` conversion.** Attach a `.claude`
64	   companion repo + `.vc-config.toml` to an existing por
65	   workspace; emit cross-links going forward. Manual
66	   setup on an external por workspace (2026-05-14)
67	   proved arduous; this should be a routine subcommand.
68	   Design stub in [[4]] § 2.
69	5. **Audit hardcoded `.claude` in diagnostics / logging.**
70	   `vc-x1 finalize --repo .bot` on `../dicom-rs`
71	   (2026-05-14) honored `--repo` throughout actual
72	   operations, but `bm-track` still emitted
73	   `.claude(main)=no-jj` — diagnostic strings have
74	   `.claude` baked in. Cosmetic today; load-bearing once
75	   bot-repo name becomes configurable (see Symmetric
76	   `.vc-config.toml` schema entry below).
77	6. **por/dual parity + `dual → por` conversion.** Make
78	   `por` and `dual` first-class equals (dual is primary
79	   today, por bolted on); add `dual → por` conversion
80	   (detach the `.claude` companion). Builds on the
81	   `--scope` rollout below. Pre-design; goal + open
82	   questions in the stub. [[4]]
83	7. **forks-multi-user + bot-data-formats follow-through.**
84	   Design captured across two notes; concrete work to
85	   land when a cycle picks it up. Major pieces:
86	   multi-line `ochid:` parser/emitter; bot-side
87	   immutability enforcement; URL-shaped ochid (per-user
88	   / cross-repo); vendor-subdir layout
89	   (`.bot/<vendor>/<version>/<id>.<ext>`) +
90	   flat-to-vendor migration; `.claude/` → `.bot/` rename
91	   (gated behind symmetric `.vc-config.toml` schema).
92	   Each piece is its own future TODO when the design
93	   hardens. [[5]],[[6]]
94	8. **Symmetric `.vc-config.toml` schema.** Add `code = "/"`
95	   and `bot = "/.claude"` (workspace-root-relative paths) so
96	   both repos read from the same shape. Side detection walks
97	   up from cwd via existing `find_workspace_root`, then maps
98	   cwd-relative-to-root onto the configured `code` / `bot`
99	   paths. Cwd-basename match is a fast-path shortcut at
100	   role-root level only — subdirs need the walk-up. Was the
101	   deferred half of `0.41.1-6`'s title. Migration story for
102	   existing workspaces TBD at design time. [[7]]
103	9. **`test_helpers::Fixture` migration + downstream callers.**
104	   Plus rename `Fixture` → `TestFixtureDual` and `FixturePor`
105	   → `TestFixturePor` so call sites carry the test-only
106	   signal that `#[cfg(test)] mod test_helpers` doesn't
107	   communicate. Was `0.41.1-7`. [[8]]
108	10. **`vc-x1 finalize --scope` flag.** Replace `--repo`
109	    with the role vocabulary used elsewhere
110	    (`code|bot|code,bot`). Carry-over from the 0.42.0
111	    `--scope` sweep (was 0.42.0-5; deferred at -4.7
112	    close-out). The paired `Single(_)` dogfood item
113	    (0.42.0-7) is moot after `0.53.0` — `Single(_)`
114	    deleted. Design lives in chores-07. [[9]]
115	11. Cross-file `chores-NN.md` ordering sanity pass.
116	    `chores-08.md` (the 0.41.1 cycle) landed on `main` via
117	    the `0.42.0-4.7` rebase; check that section ordering
118	    across `chores-06`/`-07`/`-08`/`-09` is chronologically
119	    coherent and normalize if not. Low priority.
120	12. Add a vc-x1 validate-repo?
121	13. vc-x1 push: rework the two bookmark parameters.
122	    `PushArgs` has `bookmark_pos` (positional `BOOKMARK`) +
123	    `bookmark` (`--bookmark` flag) for one logical value,
124	    forcing an `or_else` in `From<&PushArgs>`. Collapse to a
125	    single positional with `--bookmark` as a true clap alias,
126	    or drop one spelling. [[10]]
127	14. vc-x1 push: `--recheck` — implement or remove. Parsed by
128	    `PushArgs`, never read; mirrored into `PushParams` with
129	    `#[allow(dead_code)]`. Either wire the
130	    skip-preflight-on-resume behavior or drop the flag. [[11]]
131	15. vc-x1 push: `--scope=code|bot|code,bot` flag.
132	    Was 0.42.0-4 (deferred when cycle pivoted to icr
133	    rebase work; cycle closed at -4.7). State machine
134	    becomes scope-aware — single-side scope skips
135	    `commit-claude`/bookmark-claude/`finalize-claude`.
136	    [[12]],[[13]],[[14]],[[15]]
137	16. vc-x1 clone: `--scope=code|bot|code,bot` flag.
138	    Parallel to `init --scope` for role selection;
139	    topology (`--por` vs dual) is the separate `--por`
140	    boolean. Was 0.42.0-6 (deferred at -4.7
141	    close-out). [[13]],[[14]],[[15]]
142	17. vc-x1 validate-desc / fix-desc:
143	    `--scope=code|bot|code,bot` flag. Same role vocabulary
144	    as elsewhere — `code` validates code's commits against
145	    bot, `bot` reverses, `code,bot` does both (new
146	    default). [[13]],[[14]],[[15]]
147	18. Unify `.vc-config.toml` accessors onto Pattern B
148	    (typed struct + `load_from(path)`, like new
149	    `config::UserConfig` and `push::resolve_state_layout`).
150	    Replaces the map-typed helpers in `desc_helpers.rs` /
151	    `fix_desc.rs` / `validate_desc.rs` with a typed

153	    Candidate for 0.41.2. [[16]]
154	19. Layered config precedence (user → workspace → CLI)
155	    once `WorkspaceConfig` is typed. Workspace can

157	    init can't use the layer (chicken-and-egg) but

## feat: validate-todo + fix-todo (0.55.0)

Cycle close-out. The `validate-todo` / `fix-todo` subcommand
pair — a Rust replacement for `notes/fix-todo.py` — is in:
`validate-todo` checks `## Todo` / `## Bugs` numbering and
continuation indent, `fix-todo` renumbers and re-indents
(dry-run by default). Both share the `todo_helpers` core and
print an identical per-entry report line.

### As-built ladder

- 0.55.0-0 plan + version bump + chores opener
- 0.55.0-1 add validate-todo subcommand + todo_helpers
- 0.55.0-2 add fix-todo subcommand; docs sweep; delete
  notes/fix-todo.py
- 0.55.0 close-out

### Outcome

Two subcommands and a docs sweep over three steps, no
evaluation-gate detours — the subcommand work landed as
planned.

The one reversal was the opener's workflow experiment.
`0.55.0-0`'s `### Keeping the active task in ## Todo`
switched CLAUDE.md from delete-on-pickup to a keep-#1 model
(the picked-up `## Todo` entry stays under a `### Current In
Progress` heading, linked from `## In Progress`). It did its
job — the active task stayed visible in `## Todo` for easy
dogfooding mid-cycle — but at close-out the user judged it
not worth keeping. This commit reverts CLAUDE.md to plain
delete-on-pickup. A refined idea — move the picked-up entry
*into* `## In Progress` (self-describing, no heading hack)
and capture it in the chores opener — is queued as a
`## Todo` item for a later cycle.

Dropping `## Todo` item 1 and renumbering was done with
`vc-x1 fix-todo --no-dry-run` — the cycle's own tool on its
own backlog, and the close-out dogfood.

# References
